questions.question: What is the difference between a virtual function and a pure virtual function in C++? Provide an example of each.
keyPoints: Virtual function declared with 'virtual', may have a body and can be overridden in derived classes.
Pure virtual function declared with '= 0' has no implementation (unless provided separately) and must be overridden; the class becomes abstract.
An abstract class cannot be instantiated but can provide a concrete implementation for a pure virtual function.
Pure virtual functions enforce a contract that derived classes must implement the interface.

questions.question: Explain the diamond problem in multiple inheritance and how virtual inheritance resolves it.
keyPoints: Diamond problem arises when a class inherits from two classes that both inherit from a common base, creating ambiguity in the most derived class.
Virtual inheritance makes the common base a single shared subobject in the most derived class.
Declare the intermediate base classes with the 'virtual' keyword, e.g., class Middle1 : virtual public Base {}.
The most derived class is responsible for initializing the virtual base directly in its constructor.

questions.question: Write a C++ program that demonstrates runtime polymorphism using a base class pointer to derived class objects. Include a virtual function and a virtual destructor, and show how overriding works and why the virtual destructor is necessary for proper cleanup.
keyPoints: Define a base class with a virtual function (e.g., 'void display()') and a virtual destructor.
Create two derived classes that override the 'display()' function.
In main, create derived objects, store their addresses in a base class pointer, and call 'display()' to observe runtime dispatch.
Demonstrate that without a virtual destructor, only the base destructor runs, causing potential resource leaks.

questions.question: Discuss how the vtable and vptr are used to implement runtime polymorphism in C++. Explain the steps that occur when a virtual function is called through a base class pointer. Also, describe the problem of object slicing and how it can be avoided.
keyPoints: Each class with virtual functions possesses a vtable—a static array of function pointers to the most derived implementations.
Every object of such a class contains a hidden vptr that points to the class's vtable.
When a virtual function is invoked via a base pointer, the runtime uses the object's vptr to locate the correct entry in the vtable and dispatches the function.
Object slicing occurs when a derived object is assigned to a base object by value, discarding the vptr and virtual behavior; avoid slicing by passing objects by reference or pointer.
